title: NOC 94124 – Woodworking machine operators | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s secondary wood manufacturing sector—producing furniture, cabinetry, and structural components for the modular housing boom—is leaning heavily on Woodworking Machine Operators (NOC 94124). As the industry integrates Industry 4.0 technologies, these operators are transitioning from traditional manual tools to managing high-precision CNC (Computer Numerical Control) machining centers and automated finishing lines. With a "Balanced" to "Shortage" rating in major manufacturing hubs, Canada is actively recruiting international operators who can blend traditional craftsmanship with digital manufacturing skills.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group941 – Processing and manufacturing machine operators

duties: Machine Operation: Setting up and operating one or more woodworking machines (saws, routers, planers, shapers) to manufacture wood products.
CNC Programming & Setup: Loading digital files into CNC routers and adjusting tool paths for precision cutting and drilling.
Quality Verification: Inspecting wood products for defects and measuring finished pieces with calipers and gauges to ensure they match blueprints.
Tool Maintenance: Cleaning and lubricating machinery and replacing worn-out saw blades or router bits.
2026 Update: Using augmented reality (AR) overlays to verify assembly alignments and monitor material waste through digital tracking software.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is typically required.
Experience: Previous experience as a labourer in a woodworking shop is an asset; however, many employers provide training for entry-level operator roles.
Technical Skills: Basic computer literacy is mandatory for CNC-based roles.
Training: Several months of on-the-job training are standard for specialized machinery.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) expects over 14,000 job openings in this category through 2033. While automation is increasing efficiency, the complexity of custom wood manufacturing ensures that skilled operators who can manage digital systems remain in high demand.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is driven by Canada's National Housing Strategy and the demand for pre-fabricated building components:
Ontario (GTA & Kitchener): Very Good prospects. This region is Canada’s furniture manufacturing heartland, currently facing a significant shortage of CNC wood machinists.
British Columbia: Strong demand. Focused on high-end cabinetry and structural wood products for the Pacific Northwest market.
Quebec: Good prospects. Demand is high in the Victoriaville and Beauce regions, known for large-scale furniture production.
Manitoba: Stable demand. Driven by several major kitchen cabinet manufacturers located in the Winnipeg area.
Why Work in Canada as a Woodworking Machine Operator?
Modern Manufacturing: Access to 2026-grade technology, including 5-axis CNC routers, automated edge-banders, and robotic sanding systems.
Housing Boom Integration: Your skills are vital for the modular and mass-timber housing sectors, ensuring long-term job security.
Path to PR: Frequently included in Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) "In-Demand" streams due to the high volume of retiring workers.
Specialized Skill Development: Opportunity to bridge the gap between a "Machine Operator" and a "CNC Programmer" or "Production Lead."
pathways: TR to PR Pathway (April 2026): Woodworking machine operators are a high-priority "Essential Manufacturing" occupation for this fast-track stream.
Ontario Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): This NOC is frequently eligible for provincial nomination for those with a valid job offer in Ontario.
BC PNP Skilled Worker: A reliable route for operators with a full-time job offer in British Columbia’s thriving secondary wood processing sector.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: A streamlined pathway for semi-skilled manufacturing workers to obtain Permanent Residency.
